However, -ya may come very close to -e in fast pronunciation and is almost always transcribed as <e>, anyways, in the Chintang corpus, so be careful: most of the time <echa> represents a normal perfect and should thus be glossed -eko ch -a. Description: COP copula; happen; become; exist; be.there; be.good; be.possible
Comment: The morphology of this verb is highly complex. It has three senses that are expressed by a single stem in some tenses but by two or three in others: 'be', 'be there' (+ 'be' with adjectives), and 'become, be okay'. In addition, it is the only verb that has separate forms for a habitual nonpast tense parallel to the habitual past. The stem hu- with normal inflection is used for the habitual nonpast and past of all senses as well as for the nonpast of 'become'. The stem hu- with irregular inflection (suffixes directly after stem with no TMA markers) is used for 'be' in the nonpast and for the probable future and nonfinite forms of all senses. Suppletive stems are used in the past of 'become' (bhʌ-), in the past of 'be' and 'be there' (thi-), and in the nonpast of 'be there' (ch-).

Expanded_Definition_English: This word serves a great many functions, the common denominator of which is something like non-specific categorisation.
